data.title: ConocoPhillips: Iran War To Be A Major Profit Catalyst
data.description:
ConocoPhillips (COP) is a leading U.S. energy enterprise with key operations in the Lower 48 (Permian, Eagle Ford, Bakken) and a specific focus on upstream operations... which makes the firm's earnings especially sensitive to changes in petroleum prices. As opposed to Exxon Mobil (XOM) or Chevron (CVX), which are both integrated energy companies with significant downstream and midstream operational footprints, ConocoPhilips is almost entirely focused on the production of crude oil which makes the company a top bet on rising petroleum prices throughout 2026. In my opinion, the U.S.-Iran conflict could last a whole longer than the market realizes after recent peace negotiations failed, which could present a powerful earnings catalyst for ConocoPhillips and its shares.

Previous coverage

I covered ConocoPhillips last in September 2025 -- Growth, Synergies, Cheap Valuation -- and rated shares a strong buy due to the oil and gas company disposing of more assets, raising cash for expansion and up-lifting overall production due to previous Permian-centric acquisitions. Since then, shares have soared more than 32%, with boosters kicking in especially at the end of February and throughout March as the market reacted to higher oil prices resulting from the U.S.’s war with Iran. With recent peace negotiations breaking down, I believe there is a significant risk of higher-for-longer oil prices, which would obviously a potent driver of earnings growth for ConocoPhillips.

Iran war context and earnings upside

The conflict between the U.S. and Israel on one side, and Iran on the other, resulted in the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz -- is one of the most vital oil arteries and chokepoints in the world -- causing petroleum prices to soar. The Strait of Hormuz handles roughly 20-21% of global petroleum trade each day, plus a considerable volume of LNG shipments despatched from Gulf countries like Qatar. Increasing supply uncertainty has pushed petroleum prices to more than $100 per barrel, although they have recently started to drop off a little, mainly in hopes of new peace negotiations between the U.S. and Iran.

ConocoPhillips' total blended realized crude oil price in the fourth-quarter was only $42.46/BOE which means that the company will be looking forward to disclosing a serious bump in Q1'26 earnings. According to the company's sensitivity table, ConocoPhillips expects a $1/BOE price change to have a $140-150M earnings impact, on a full-year basis. With crude oil prices rising to $95-96/BOE now, the company could be set to report an average realized crude oil price of ~$72/BOE. This price is about $7.19/BOE higher than the baseline price reported by COP in its sensitivity table.

Applying the incremental $7.19/BOE price gain to a total production volume of 2,320 MBOED, the firm could achieve incremental net earnings of $1.04B (full-year) and ~$260M for Q1'26. This windfall in profits could yield either a dividend raise or an increase in stock buybacks.

ConocoPhillips is obviously a much smaller company than either Exxon Mobil or Chevron, but it did buy a lot of stock back in recent years nonetheless, mainly due to stronger-than-expected free cash flow following Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022. In the fourth-quarter, ConocoPhillips reported free cash flow of $1.3B which calculates to a free cash flow return percentage of 162%.

Much of this free cash flow ended up in the pockets of shareholders in the last several years, however, as the company was a prolific purchaser of its own shares in the marketplace. In the fourth-quarter of 2025, it spent about $1.0B each on stock buybacks and dividends, and the expected windfall in Q1'26 could lead to a potential acceleration of capital returns for investors in the remainder of the year.

ConocoPhillips’ valuation

Shares of Conocophillips went through a major revaluation in the last six weeks, in response to higher petroleum prices, and escalating supply fears as they relate to the blockade of the Strait of Hormuz. I also see a favorable environment for higher-for-longer crude oil prices as the conflict between the U.S. and Iran is dragging on and recent peace talks didn't yield a positive outcome.

Higher petroleum prices have already resulted in a positive revision trend for COP’s first-quarter earnings estimates: in the last 90 days, the energy enterprise was subjected to a total of 14 revisions, 10 of which were to the upside, and only 4 were (older) downside revisions. The market now currently expects earnings of $1.68 per-share which highlights a 20% decrease compared to the year-earlier period which is when oil prices were even higher than they are now.

Shares of ConocoPhillips are priced at 15.4X earnings for FY 2027, only slightly (3%) above the energy firm's 3-year average price-to-earnings ratio; however, I feel that this P/E ratio undervalues the earnings boost the company is set to report for Q1'26.

Exxon Mobil and Chevron, two of the largest energy companies not just in the U.S. but worldwide, are trading at P/E ratios of 15.5X and 17.9X. Arguably, ConocoPhillips has both more upside and downside potential compared to its peers, which is due to the company's more concentrated profile in upstream operations. I believe that ConocoPhillips could trade at a ~17-18X earnings multiplier given its more aggressive earnings profile... which implies a fair value between $136 and $144 per-share.

Risks with ConocoPhillips

The biggest risk for the oil and gas producer relates to a severe drop-off in petroleum prices which would immediately affect ConocoPhillips' earnings potential for the remainder of the year. Since ConocoPhillips is more vulnerable to earnings swings than Exxon Mobil or Chevron, given its core focus on oil and gas production, a downturn in energy prices would affect the energy price much more severely. What would change my mind about COP in addition to falling average prices is if the company were not to accelerate its capital returns (stock buybacks) in 2026.

Final thoughts

ConocoPhillips could be heading for a massive earnings report on April 30, 2026 which is when the energy firm is scheduled to release first-quarter results. The oil and gas company is benefiting from a resetting of price realizations to the upside and with crude oil currently trading at $95-96/BOE, I believe Chevron is going to see a ~$260M earnings tailwind in the first-quarter.

I like ConocoPhillips especially as an aggressive play on petroleum prices which, in my opinion, are going to increase going forward, not decrease. The recent failure of peace negotiations between the U.S. and Iran in Pakistan highlights that the U.S. may be in for a longer conflict in the Middle East which creates long term pricing upside for companies like COP.

In my opinion, ConocoPhillips is better positioned to capture this incremental pricing and earnings upside compared to Exxon Mobil and Chevron which are more diversified and can mitigate negative price impacts through their downstream operations.

data.title: 7 High-Yield Fixed Income CEFs Trading At Wide Discounts
data.description:
As a now retired passive income investor who supports my lifestyle with the income generated by my investments, I would like to offer up a brief review of seven closed end funds that offer high-yield income from a mix of fixed income securities. All seven of these funds, with current yields ranging from 10% to 14%, offer steady monthly income despite prices that have been driven down to wider-than-average discounts.

With the increasing threat of inflation rearing its ugly head again and the Fed unlikely to reduce rates to stimulate growth anytime soon, now is a good time for defensive income investors to hunker down and collect the income while share prices head down. For long-term income investors, now might be a good time to open new positions or add to existing ones while the discounts remain wider than average due to market fears resulting from the private credit scare.

Investors sought to get back more than $20B of their investments from private credit firms in Q1 2026, a sign of the increasing concern after a boom in the asset class, according to a media report on Thursday.

The Financial Times calculated that the sector, including Apollo Global Management (APO), Ares Management (ARES), Blackstone (BX), Blue Owl Capital Inc. (OWL), and KKR (KKR), received $20.8B in redemption requests during the quarter.

The recent round of redemption requests underscores concerns over the private credit sector's lending to private equity-backed software companies and the uncertainty those software businesses face as artificial intelligence advances. In addition, once the Federal Reserve boosted interest rates in 2023, private equity firms have had a tougher time exiting their older leveraged buyouts. Private credit has largely financed those deals.

In my opinion, many of the fixed income funds that are offered by those asset managers, like Ares, KKR, and Blackstone, are also experiencing negative market pricing impacts as a dropdown effect from this overall fear regarding private credit. In this review, I will briefly discuss seven CEFs (closed end funds) that offer high-yield income from a combination of fixed income securities such as high yield corporate bonds, senior secured loans, MBS (mortgage-backed securities), and CLOs (collateralized loan obligations). Each of the funds in this review has seen a negative hit to its market price over the past six months that has sent the current prices to much wider than average discounts.

The 7 CEFs in this review include:

  • ARDC
  • BGH
  • BIT
  • GHY
  • KIO
  • OPP
  • WDI

The total return for each of the 7 funds has ranged from slightly negative to just barely positive over the past six months.

Before I get into the details of each fund, I wanted to discuss some of the market conditions that might favor these funds, which hold some combination of different types of fixed income securities ranging from high-yield bonds to leveraged loans (aka senior loans) as well as some CLOs. The majority of holdings in each fund typically include senior loans and/or corporate bonds.

One primary difference between those two types of securities is that leveraged loans are typically floating rate, while corporate bonds tend to be fixed rate. By holding a mix of senior loans and corporate bonds, some portfolio diversification can be achieved to offset the impacts of rising or lowering interest rates, as explained in this insight from Morningstar:

Leveraged loans and high-yield bonds offer attractive yields compared to Treasuries and investment-grade bonds, reflecting their higher credit risk. These asset classes serve as valuable tools for portfolio diversification, enhancing risk-adjusted returns. Historical performance analysis highlights that leveraged loans exhibit lower correlation to equities than high-yield bonds, making them a superior diversifier.

Using Markowitz efficient portfolio theory, studies across multiple time frames show that leveraged loans contribute to more resilient portfolios with improved risk-adjusted returns. Additionally, their floating-rate structure reduces sensitivity to interest rate fluctuations. During rate-hike cycles, leveraged loans have historically outperformed high-yield bonds, while high-yield bonds tend to deliver stronger returns in rate-cut environments. By strategically incorporating both asset classes, investors can optimize portfolio performance across different market conditions.

Senior (or leveraged) loans are also held in CLOs, and because they are secured by the company’s assets and represent the highest position in the capital stack, they are considered lower risk. This benefit is further described in this insight from Invesco:

Senior loans, also known as leveraged loans or bank loans, are debt securities utilized by companies to finance their operations, support business expansion, and refinance existing debt. They’re senior because of their uppermost position in a company's capital structure, meaning they’re the highest priority to be repaid in a default. Senior loans are typically secured by the borrower's assets, such as cash, receivables, inventory, or property, and are structured as floating-rate instruments, which means the interest paid on these loans fluctuates with interest rate changes.

CLOs are backed by a pool of senior loans and have various coverage, collateral, and quality tests that manage them. They buy the underlying senior loans by issuing different debt/equity slices with different cashflow priorities. The most senior of these slices are AAA-rated and first in line for cash flow coming into the CLO.

Other types of fixed income securities that these funds may hold include investment-grade corporate bonds, Agency MBS (mortgage-backed securities), and non-agency structured credit. According to this insight from Columbia Threadneedle, yields are higher across the board than their 20-year average while credit spreads (with the exception of Agency MBS) are much tighter than average, reflecting the heightened perceived market risk in April due to the Iran conflict. Some of the key takeaways from their insight include:

  • The Federal Reserve held rates steady at 3.5%-3.75% at its March meeting, maintaining a cautious stance amid elevated inflation concerns and heightened economic uncertainty. The Fed noted that inflation remains somewhat elevated and explicitly cited Middle East developments and rising oil prices as key risks to the economic outlook.
  • Treasury yields rose significantly through March, with the 10-year U.S. Treasury reaching its highest level since July 2025, driven by a combination of elevated oil prices from the ongoing U.S.-Iran conflict, rising inflation-expectations and diminishing near-term rate cut expectations. Rising real rates accounted for most of the increase in Treasury yields.
  • Credit markets remained resilient, with spreads widening modestly. Duration was a larger headwind than credit, as investment-grade credit spreads widened but lagged the move higher in Treasury yields.

According to Blackrock, there is a good case to be made for collecting income from fixed income investments in 2026 that include a steepening yield curve, higher yields across the board, and labor market weakness driving Fed policy, not inflation.

Rick Rieder, Chief Investment Officer of Global Fixed Income, sees labor market weakness—not inflation—as the dominant force shaping the outlook. With price pressures largely contained, labor softness is likely to drive Fed policy and keep income-oriented assets attractive.

Income delivered strongly in 2025, and Rick believes the backdrop remains supportive in 2026, creating opportunities to earn yield with limited duration risk.

Given that backdrop on macro conditions, let’s take a closer look at each of the 7 multi-asset fixed income CEFs, going in alphabetical order.

ARDC

First up is Ares Dynamic Credit Allocation Fund (ARDC). Currently, as of April 10, 2026, ARDC offers a yield of 11% and is trading at a discount to NAV (net asset value) of -9.3%.

From the fund website, here are some key facts about ARDC.

Ares Dynamic Credit Allocation Fund, Inc. (the “Fund”) is a closed-end management investment company. The Fund’s investment objective is to provide an attractive level of total return, primarily through current income and, secondarily, through capital appreciation.

From the fund fact sheet we can see that the asset mix includes about 38% loans, 34% bonds, 16% in CLO Debt and nearly 10% in CLO Equity. Roughly 60% of holdings are floating rate.

Fund performance has been very good for the past 3 years but not so great over the past 5-year period or since inception in 2012. Returns are based on 2/28/26 closing prices, according to the fact sheet.

The fund distribution history follows the trajectory of interest rates due to its substantial floating rate exposure, with distributions increasing when rates were rising, then cut when rates started dropping again.

Finally, looking at the price history relative to NAV over the past 3 years, we can see that ARDC traded at a big discount in early 2023 that closed completely and began trading at a small premium for most of 2024 before dropping back to a wide discount again during the April 2025 Tariff Tantrums. The fund traded at an average discount of -4.4% over the past year but now trades at a significantly wider discount of -9.3%. Meanwhile, the NAV has started to recover in the first two weeks of April 2026.

BGH

Next up is Barings Global Short Duration High Yield Fund (BGH). Currently trading at a discount of -6.7% this CEF now yields 10.6%. (Note: Seeking Alpha shows a yield of 12.3%, which most likely includes the special dividend of $0.241 paid in September 2025).

BGH has a similar investment objective to ARDC which includes a high level of current income with a secondary objective of capital appreciation. The fund strategy is explained on the website.

  • Fund will invest at least 80 percent of its managed assets in corporate bonds, loans and other income-producing instruments that are rated below investment grade
  • Fund may invest up to 50 percent of its managed assets in bonds and loans issued by foreign companies
  • Seek to maintain a weighted average portfolio duration of three years or less

According to the fund fact sheet, portfolio holdings include mostly secured and unsecured corporate bonds spread across a diversity of industries.

The performance has been impressive over the past three years, but like ARDC, which also launched in 2012, has delivered less than stellar average annual returns since inception.

The distribution history since inception shows a big cut after the 2020 Covid pandemic but an increase in November 2024 to $0.1223 per month, where it has remained since.

The price movement over the past three years also looks quite similar to ARDC, with a very wide discount back in April 2023 rising to a premium above 6% in 2025 before dropping back to another wider than average discount of -6.25% in April 2026.

These types of big price swings tend to be more correlated with investor sentiment or macro factors rather than interest rate movements. If we zoom into just the past one-year period, this price action relative to NAV becomes even more apparent. The NAV has barely declined in the past 12 months and remains near $15, but the price has moved from $14 to almost $17 and then back below $14.

BIT

Another multi-asset fixed income fund that offers high yield current income is BlackRock Multi-Sector Income Trust (BIT). This fund offers a current yield of 11.6% and now trades at a discount of -6%.

BIT has a similar investment approach to ARDC and BGH with holdings that include a mix of fixed income securities.

BlackRock Multi-Sector Income Trust’s (the 'Trust') primary investment objective is to seek high current income, with a secondary objective of capital appreciation. The Trust seeks to achieve its investment objectives by investing, under normal market conditions, at least 80% of its assets in loan and debt instruments and other investments with similar economic characteristics. The Trust may invest directly in such securities or synthetically through the use of derivatives.

BIT is one of the bigger funds in terms of net assets with about $775M as of April 10, 2026, and nearly 1,600 individual holdings. Other key facts about the fund are shown on the website.

BIT holds just about every type of fixed income security available, with a majority of the portfolio focused on high-yield bonds, as shown on the fact sheet.

With an inception date of 2013, BIT has delivered good performance over the past 3 years but suffered a market price decline in 2025 that left one-year performance (as of 12/31/25) in negative territory despite a 6.6% NAV return as shown on the fact sheet.

Regarding distribution history, BIT is a member of the Never Cutter club. I wrote about BIT in my recent article: Never Cutters, Part 2: 5 More High-Yield CEFs That Have Never Cut The Distribution.

Not only has BIT never cut the distribution since inception, but they have also paid out several special dividends, and increased the monthly distribution to $0.1237 back in 2019 where it has remained since.

Lastly, BIT traded at a small premium for most of the past three years, then moved to an average discount of -3% for the past year. It now trades at a much wider than average discount of -6.4%.

GHY

As suggested by the fund name, portfolio holdings include some non-US fixed income securities. PGIM Global High Yield Fund (GHY) is the next high-yield fixed income fund on our list that is trading at a wider-than-average discount of -8.8%. The current yield is 10.7% based on a monthly distribution of $0.1050.

The fund’s investment objective is a bit different from the others that we have looked at on this list. From the fund website, here are some key facts:

The Fund seeks to provide a high level of current income by investing primarily in below investment-grade fixed income instruments of issuers located around the world, including emerging markets.

From the fund fact sheet, the geographic concentration shows that only 43.6% of assets are US-based. Credit quality indicates that a majority of holdings are below investment grade.

Fund performance has been very good for the past three years (as of 12/31/25 fact sheet). Since inception in December 2012, the fund returned 5.6% annually through 2025, roughly comparable to the others on this list.

The fund distribution history has been consistent since 2019, with $0.1050 paid monthly.

And like the others on this list, the fund moved in price from a very wide discount in April 2023 to a small premium in 2025 before returning to a discount, now -8.3%. That is more than 5 percentage points wider than the one-year average discount of about -3%.

KIO

Let’s next take a look at KKR Income Opportunities Fund (KIO). Now trading at a discount to NAV of -7.9% the current yield is 13.3%.

The fund’s investment strategy is more macro driven than some of its peers as explained on the fund website:

KKR Income Opportunities Fund (“KIO” or “the Fund”) seeks to allocate across credit instruments to capitalize on changes in relative value among corporate credit investments and manage against macroeconomic risks.

The fund attempts to achieve its goal by holding a targeted portfolio that primarily invests in a mix of senior loans, high-yield bonds, and CLOs. As of 12/31/25 the portfolio composition shows a nearly equal allocation to leveraged loans and high-yield bonds with about 10% CLOs.

Fund performance has been similar to the peers on this list with a strong 3-year total return through the end of 2025, but only about 6% annual returns since the fund’s inception date of 2013 as shown on the fact card.

The fund has paid a regular monthly distribution with just one cut in 2020 from $0.1250 to $0.1050 monthly, which was subsequently increased again to $0.1215 monthly in March 2023, where it has remained since.

The price history has also followed a familiar pattern over the past three years, with a wide discount in April 2023 increasing to a small premium in mid-2024 and then falling back to a wider-than-average discount in the first quarter of 2026.

OPP

The next CEF to review is a unique fund with two portfolio managers, RiverNorth/DoubleLine Strategic Opportunity Fund (OPP). Offering a current yield of 14%, OPP now trades at a discount of -9.4% (about 2.5% wider than the one-year average discount). OPP uses a managed distribution policy that sets the monthly distribution in January at an amount equal to 12.5% of the average of the NAV per share based on the final five trading days of the prior year. That same amount is paid each of the next twelve months and the fund resets again the following January.

I last covered OPP in November 2024 when they announced a rights offering. Some key facts from the fact sheet show an inception date of September 2016.

This is a rather unique fund of funds that holds mostly fixed income holdings, along with a smattering of other credit and equity investments including ETFs, BDCs, SPACs (special purpose acquisition companies), MBS (mortgage-backed securities), and other CEFs. From the latest fund fact sheet, here is the full description of the fund’s investment strategy:

RiverNorth allocates the Fund’s assets among three principal strategies: Tactical Closed End Fund Income Strategy, Alternative Credit Strategy, and Opportunistic Income Strategy. RiverNorth manages the Tactical CEF Income Strategy and the Alternative Credit Strategy, DoubleLine manages the Opportunistic Income Strategy. RiverNorth determines which portion of the Fund’s assets is allocated to each strategy based on market conditions.

The portfolio is currently about 75% weighted by Double Line with its mostly MBS holdings.

Fund performance as of 12/31/25 from the fact sheet shows good 3-year total returns at both NAV and market price but poor total returns for the last 5 years and since inception.

Distributions were rising for the first three years after fund inception but have been on the decline since 2020.

This indicates some NAV erosion has contributed to this trend over the past 5 years; however, macro conditions have been changing with respect to MBS securities. The current environment favors MBS securities as suggested in this insight from AllianceBernstein.

Although it has traded at a discount for the past three years, OPP has seen its discount widen even further over the past six months. Assuming the fund can resume some NAV growth in the coming months, the current discount may represent an opportunity to acquire reasonably priced shares of a fund that offers reliable monthly income and could offer some capital appreciation whenever the discount closes.

WDI

Last on this list is a diversified fixed income CEF, Western Asset Diversified Income Fund (WDI). WDI offers a current yield of 13% and trades at a -5.5% discount.

WDI is the newest of the multi-sector bond funds, with an inception date of June 2021. Top sectors held by the fund as shown on the website include high-yield credit, CLOs, CMBS, and Bank Loans.

The fund is income-focused, and key facts are shown on the fact sheet.

Fund performance is limited to less than 5 years, making comparisons difficult, but the past 3 years have been strong years, while 2022 was a disaster, dragging down the long-term results as shown on the fact sheet.

Although the distribution history is also relatively short, it indicates several increases and no cuts since fund inception in 2021.

Probably the best indicator that WDI may offer a potential buying opportunity at the current discount is this 3-year chart showing a similar pattern to the other funds on this list. It started at a wide discount in April 2023 that narrowed and briefly traded at a slight premium before dropping again to a wider than average discount. Now the trend appears to be reversing, with NAV on the rise again in early April 2026.

This concludes my review of 7 high-yield, fixed-income CEFs that offer potential short-term buying opportunities for long-term-focused passive income investors.

Strategy

Vanguard ESG International Stock ETF (VSGX) was launched on 09/18/2018 and tracks the FTSE Global All Cap ex US Choice Index. VSGX has a portfolio of 6588 stocks, a 12-month trailing yield of 3.10%, and an expense ratio of 0.10%.

As described by Vanguard, the underlying index includes stocks of ex-U.S. developed markets and emerging markets meeting certain environmental, social, and corporate governance ("ESG") criteria. Companies are screened based on their involvement in the following product and service categories: alcohol, tobacco, cannabis, gambling, chemical and biological weapons, cluster munitions, anti-personnel mines, nuclear weapons, conventional military weapons, civilian firearms, nuclear power, coal, oil, or gas. The measure and threshold of involvement leading to exclusion may vary from one category to another. Companies that do not meet certain labor, human rights, environmental, anti-corruption, and diversity policy standards may also be excluded. The index is weighted based on market capitalization.

The fund's turnover rate in the most recent fiscal year was 8%. I will use as a benchmark the FTSE Global All Cap ex US Index, represented by the Vanguard Total International Stock Index Fund ETF (VXUS).

The geographical allocation is close to the benchmark's, although VSGX overweights Taiwan and moderately downplays the U.K. and Canada.

The portfolio has significant exposure in financials (26%) and technology (19%). Other sectors are below 10%. Compared to the benchmark, VSGX overweights mostly these two sectors and downplays industrials, utilities, and energy.

data.title: A Legendary Investor Is Gone - But His Strategy Might Matter More Than Ever
data.description:
Introduction

But I'll say this, as most of you probably know, I spent decades trying to convince people to be long-term investors, and I haven't convinced one person yet. They all want to trade. - Murray Stahl (August 19, 2025), via UnhedgedChatter.

I have often said that the stock market has become incredibly tricky. In fact, it’s the main reason why I do the work that I do, as we are dealing with so many factors that have turned what used to be rather straightforward business cycles into complex puzzles.

Inflation is still a problem, geopolitical tensions have been sky-high for years, including multiple wars that have all impacted the market and economic numbers, we’re dealing with unprecedented disruption risks from AI, and countless other things that are all connected in some way.

Dealing with all of this requires a solid strategy and an even stronger mindset. A big part of my investment journey was following Murray Stahl. What Warren Buffett is to many is what Murray Stahl was to me, a very successful investor I admired for his approach.

As you may have noticed, I used the past tense here, as Murray Stahl sadly and unexpectedly passed away on April 7. According to the Seeking Alpha news article:

Horizon Kinetics (HKHC) on Wednesday announced with deep sorrow that Murray Stahl, the company's chairman, and chief executive officer, passed away suddenly on April 7, 2026.

Murray co-founded Horizon Kinetics in 1994. He was instrumental in transforming it into a publicly traded company with a market capitalization of approximately $600M, an impeccable and debt-free balance sheet, and a portfolio built for the long horizon. Murray's dedication, wisdom, and leadership will be felt for generations to come, the company said. - Seeking Alpha.

If you are familiar with my work, you know that I have used various aspects of his research and approach in my work, including the Equity Yield Curve, which I covered in an in-depth article last year. This model is based on the theory that short-term-focused markets offer long-term opportunities because most investors are focused on chasing short-term performance for various reasons, while the financial media also tends to care more about catchy headlines about current developments than “boring” long-term theses.

Needless to say, I am painting with a broad brush, but that’s the gist of it.

Moreover, we had some things in common, including our love for landowners and stock exchanges, which are both “toll-booth-like” businesses. This includes Texas Pacific Land (TPL), a stock I bought before I was familiar with Murray Stahl and LandBridge (LB), a stock I bought the day after its IPO when I heard Stahl mention it in an investor round table.

Both stocks sold off aggressively, with Texas Pacific Land falling more than 15.0%.

This decline created one of my worst days in years from a financial point of view, as the market seems to be afraid that with Murray Stahl no longer being a Board member of Texas Pacific, the company may not be so focused on shareholder value anymore. As I will explain in future articles (this is not a TPL article), I do not believe that will be the case.

We need to be aware that Murray Stahl built a strong team over the years, including people like Steven Bregman, Peter Doyle, Tom Ewing, and research analysts like James Davolos, with whom I agree on many things, including how to build an inflation-resistant portfolio.

In other words, when I praise Murray Stahl in this article, we always need to view it in the context of the bigger team, as we can assume major investment decisions were made in a team setting, so to speak.

With that said, this article is all about what I learned, how this can help us build portfolios, and why I admired this approach so much, especially for an environment like the one we’re currently in.

Even if you have never heard of Mr. Stahl, I believe you’ll learn a lot today. At least, that’s the goal.

So, let’s dive in!

The Realization That We’re In A “New Normal”

At this point, I need to mention that I didn’t just copy and paste Murray Stahl. What I liked about him is that he had done so much work on things that I agree with as well, including our love for specific business models. For example, I was long CME Group (CME) way before I found out about how Horizon Kinetics used stock exchanges.

And, even more important, I have no ties with Horizon Kinetics in any way. That’s crucial for me to mention, as I am saying a lot of good things about a company that offers its own investment services and ETFs. As much as I would have liked to meet Mr. Stahl and his team, I doubt they know who I am.

Anyway, what I liked so much is how they always include the Big Picture in their approach. For example, for more than 30 years, the market has been a rather good place to be, even if it has had a number of steep selloffs. This includes a long-term decline in real interest rates, mostly favorable inflation tailwinds, consistently rising corporate profits, and long-term productivity benefits (think of the computer and the Internet going mainstream, wireless connectivity, and AI, among other things).

Also, government finances were way more favorable. For a few years, we’ve been dealing with consistently elevated deficits and a debt-to-GDP ratio in the triple-digit range. I don’t want to take too many detours, but just to give you another example of why this matters, the elevated deficit and debt load make surges in interest rates an even bigger risk.

It all adds to macro and market complexities that I discuss almost on a daily basis.

Now, the situation is way more complex. Although I am not bearish on the market, my view is that we are likely to see subdued returns in the years ahead, as inflation is now consistently above the Fed’s target, geopolitical risks are elevated, supply chains are suffering from deglobalization, as economic re-shoring is now more important than chasing high margins (from a nation’s point-of-view), energy supply growth is less rapid, while demand is booming, and so many other factors are playing a role.

That’s why I have been a big believer in the “run it hot” thesis, which is based on my belief (supported by our intel) that the government will accept higher inflation if it leads to accelerating and more broad-based nominal growth. So far, that seems to be the case.

However, it also means that the portfolio constructions that worked so well since the Great Financial Crisis may not work so well anymore going forward.

A New Approach For Long-Term Wealth

If there’s one thing I often say, it’s that I’m a long-term investor. I do not care about short-term developments, and I always focus on assets that can generate wealth for many years to come, even if I have a specific thesis in mind.

I want to create durable portfolios instead of creating a scenario where we all end up owning “event-driven” portfolios. For some, that may work, but for most, that’s not the best way.

What Murray Stahl understood way before I knew how to spell “stock market” is that these long-term compounders have very specific characteristics. It’s why he did not invest in traditional oil and gas companies (at least, they were not among his holdings when he passed).

Murray Stahl (and his team) cared a lot about capital expenditures and inflation. For example, as bullish as I may be on some oil and gas producers (I own Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ)), these companies require a lot of capital to operate. They invest in machinery, materials, labor, and so many other things. The same goes for mining companies. When commodity prices rise, they benefit. However, they also deal with input inflation that can pressure margins.

Instead, they went for “capital light” companies that are part of “fully-cycle” real estate exposure. It consists of companies with real assets that focus on areas with limited supply, capital-light business models that allow for compounding shareholder wealth over time, and attractive valuations that are based on more than favorable P/E ratios.

Essentially, they want to own “the house.” They called them “croupiers” in their Q4 '25 letter (and in various research pieces before that). Think of a company like CME Group (CME). This company is the largest futures exchange operator on the planet. Every time someone trades major futures contracts on WTI crude oil, NYMEX natural gas, copper, gold, silver, the S&P 500, and other assets, CME makes money. This business model is easy to scale, as trading is done electronically. Also, launching new products is often based on licenses and regulatory filings. It doesn’t require CME to build a factory or a new office.

And then there’s Texas Pacific Land, which owns almost 900 thousand surface acres in the Permian Basin. It has surface, water, and mineral rights, which means that it makes money on everything that happens on its land and under the ground. Oil companies pay royalties in exchange for drilling rights, while others pay for the right to pump produced water into the ground. It’s also working with hyperscalers to bring data centers to its land and provide the power for them.

All of this happens with close to nothing in CapEx, as oil companies are responsible for their own expenses. This provides double inflation protection.

Just like a real croupier in a casino, they take a cut without taking on the risk.

I truly wish I had come up with that, as the framework shows a powerful “flywheel effect.”

There’s one thing I did come up with, though.

And, on a side note, while I tend to follow the croupier model, I still invested in Canadian Natural Resources, as I already briefly mentioned. This company may be an oil producer, yet it owns oil sand operations in Canada. These are expensive operations, yet most costs are upfront, as the mining infrastructure needs to be built. Once that’s taken care of, they enjoy very low operating costs and massive reserves without decline rates.

It’s the croupier mindset applied in a different setting.

Focusing On The Equity Yield Curve

My personal strategy is focused on taking concentrated positions in high-conviction ideas that have a clear strategic advantage, including energy (landowners, mostly), defense, logistics, and construction suppliers (not asset-heavy construction companies).

And, just like Murray Stahl and his team, I focus on long-term opportunities that are somewhat hidden. Last year was a great example of that. While the market, in general, was focused on AI, I bought unloved energy stocks and cyclical value, as I expected an upswing in leading growth indicators.

While I make mistakes, that wasn’t a mistake, as this year, so far, has seen outperformance of cyclical value, energy (the S&P 500 leader), and an upswing in economic growth expectations (i.e., the ISM Manufacturing Index).

I did the same in 2020/2021. We want to buy high-quality businesses when they are unloved, simply because the market is occupied with something else. On the flip side, we also avoided software due to disruption risks. It took a while for that thesis to unfold, yet AI was the main factor that showed how rapidly a competitive moat can evaporate.

When combining this investment approach with the croupier/asset-light framework, we can create ultra-resilient portfolios that are not only perfect for what I expect to be a lasting change in the investment environment, but also protect us in case we’re wrong, as most companies that I own also did well over the past ten years. It will just make it harder for me to outperform. But in general, I love the risk/reward, and the chart below shows why.

Moreover, I expanded a bit on this model, as I built the TOLL model, something I am increasingly using in my regular articles. I introduced this concept in an article titled “Forget FANG, I’m Betting On ‘TOLL’ Stocks Instead.”

It is very similar to the CapEx-light model, except that it makes exceptions. And, to be fair, it was initially based on the HALO model that was introduced by Ritholz CEO Josh Brown.

This model is based on finding companies with tangible assets (the T) that have limited competition (the O of oligopoly), low-incremental capital intensity (the first L), and long-duration cash flows (the second L). For example, technically speaking, the croupier model does not favor railroads or midstream companies, as these require elevated capital to build rail and pipeline networks. That’s one of the reasons why the midstream industry, in general, struggled so much to generate shareholder value before the pandemic, as CapEx requirements were simply overwhelming.

Think of Enterprise Products Partners (EPD). Although it has one of the best track records in its industry, once the shale revolution gained momentum and massive CapEx for infrastructure was needed, its stock wasn’t popular.

However, once these companies have their assets in place, they start to generate income with limited incremental CapEx. That’s favorable for margins and free cash flow, and one of the main reasons why I came up with the TOLL model.

It’s also why I stick to what I wrote in my first TOLL article:

  • I invest in cyclical value like Union Pacific and Old Dominion to benefit from a cyclical discount. In other words, I take advantage of the time discount before earnings normalization. I would never ever use lower-quality companies for this thesis, as I want the timing of the cyclical recovery to be my main risk, not the survivability of my holdings.
  • I like a mix of upstream energy (producers) and midstream (pipelines) to benefit from energy normalization. The high equity yields provide investors with income, while others cannot wait for energy normalization.
  • My general dividend growth thesis is based on using time in the market for compounding purposes, something most funds cannot do due to the reasons mentioned in this article.

And, to go back to the article where I first discussed the Equity Yield Curve, I also noted that I had to carefully communicate my strategy in my articles. Murray Stahl was known for holding high-conviction positions through many cycles. It’s why he became a billionaire, as he stuck to high-conviction investments and didn’t take profits on things that kept working.

On Seeking Alpha, that is harder, as we all have different strategies, goals, and ages. I cannot possibly tell an 85-year-old to buy stock XYZ with a holding period of at least 20 years. That’s another reason why I use TOLL, as most asset-light compounders lack the income that many investors need. Midstream stocks, like EPD, may lack certain asset-light benefits, yet they provide the income many need and other TOLL benefits that make them suitable long-term investors.

Takeaway

Murray Stahl’s passing is a huge loss for the financial community, especially because many of his teachings seem to be more important than ever right now. As I have mentioned this so much, I could do it in my sleep, because we are dealing with countless challenges and structural changes that make it very likely that the next decade looks very different from the last.

That requires potential changes without the risks of us turning our portfolios into trading vehicles. After all, we want long-term compounding wealth, not actively managing our wealth.

Findings like the Equity Yield Curve can help us find long-term opportunities, while a focus on asset-light croupiers (or the TOLL model) provides support for asset selection.

It also brought me to my slogan:

“Time is the edge, quality is the filter, valuation is the trigger.”

In other words, I approach markets with a simple focus, which is to focus on durable compounders, buy them when they are unloved, and then let time in the market do the rest.
